title: What I think about the Future of the Media

transcript.text: Hello, this video is what I think about the future of the media.
I did another video before what I think about the media, but this is, I guess, part two where I talk about the future.
And maybe I'm saying the same things, but I guess I'm saying it in a slightly different way.
Basically, what's going to happen with the future of the media, and it's also the future of everything, is going to follow this pattern.
Decentralization.
That's the big key word.
And you just follow the pattern from the beginning.
With truth in the beginning, all of Europe would follow one guy, the Pope.
And then it got a little bit more decentralized, all of a country would follow one guy, the Bishop.
Or politically, you could say, follow one guy, the Emperor, and then the country, follow one guy, the King.
And nowadays, we've got this very amazing phenomenon happening, YouTube, where people can have, everybody has their own channel.
So you've got, like, the four major channels, CBS, ABC, NBC, and whatever the other one is.
And you've got the Fox and all these guys.
And then you've got all these individual channels.
And over the last couple of years, or the last year, it's just really mushroomed.
People are starting to make money off of their own channels with AdSense, with Google, being partners.
And people are going to start to watch less and less TV.
I don't know what the statistics are, but just my common sense told me that that's the case.
Newspapers are going out of business.
They're going to go out of business because they just take up too many resources, too expensive to maintain and to buy.
And they just make too much mess in your house.
And it's also centralized, so it's all going to become decentralized.
And we're always going to have, you know, online newspapers and such.
But more and more people are going to be interested in individual people.
And so instead of, you know, education is going to be the same way.
But instead of seeing certain people getting all of the airplay because they're established and they got established that way.
And then so other people would get their airplay by going under the wing of the mama bird and then following mama bird's rules and going up.
People can start wherever they want, follow their own rules.
And it's a complete 100% beautiful meritocracy where they would grow up and become more powerful based on their merit, based on people's interest in them.
Individuals decide to subscribe to somebody.
Individuals decide to click on somebody's video.
And so, yeah, there's always going to be people on the top who have the best news.
And then if they stop being so good, then somebody else who does better is going to.
And people will get their news that way.
So there'll be all kind of different kinds of specialized channels like that.
So I think eventually it's all going to be online and it's all going to be basically like YouTube is now.
Now, of course, you're going to people like leaders.
So there is always going to be leaders with other people under their wing, kind of affiliated with them.
Like, say, maybe somebody starts up now making a news channel and then they get really popular.
But I think the revenue is always going to be from advertising, from their videos.
And so they're going to make their money from the clicks and stuff and subscriptions and everything.
So I guess, you know, as far as will it be like a newspaper, like where other people would be affiliated and like.
Well, I think only in such that they would have like recommended channels.
And that's what we have already with subscribers.
The subscribers can recommend each other's channelers.
So nobody will, um, will work for anybody.
There will, there won't be that, uh, you know, this is Jimmy John's channel.
And then, you know, there's, uh, and then, you know, Jimmy John has, uh, Johnny Joe out there in field, doing the field for him.
He just has, uh, the freelancer, which I guess Jimmy John uses.
But I guess they, newspapers already use that.
You have the freelance journalists who use the newspapers.
So anyways, um, um, I heard that Saudi Arabians are using all their money to buy up all of our media.
And, uh, um, they have, are doing that in America and also in Europe.
Um, I heard, I, what was it?
Chris Condell said the other day that they had just made, um, um, um, uh, slandering your government illegal, officially illegal there.
And it was the, uh, Muslims who did that.
And I remember five years ago there was that, uh, what was it?
The Danish, uh, cartoonist, uh, drew a picture of Mohammed in one of his, uh, cartoons.
And he was fired because the newspaper he worked for was actually owned by Muslims.
And I have heard that, um, that English teachers who go to Saudi Arabia are told by their students that they don't care about learning English anyway because in 30 years everybody will speak Arabic.
Uh, and they think they're going to take over the whole world because they're, like, superior because they're, they're, uh, Islam.
Um, and there's obviously, uh, definitely a faction who believes that, um, just look at the terrorists.

But, um, anyways, to those people I would say, you know, thanks for investing in our country, but you're not going to get anywhere because the media is eventually going to, um, these big media companies are eventually going to, um, dissolve.

They're going to get less and less popular, especially, um, the, the way that people do, um, the way that our media works now, you have these people talking, basically judging each other's, what people's, how people say stuff instead, rather than what they say it.
Now, I'm not really, don't really have that much of a leg to stand on.
I'm just kind of using my, uh, just my gut feeling because I never watch the news ever.
And I never pay attention to what's going on.
I really don't.
So I'm really an ignoramus on that.
I, I'm only really interested in issues and stuff.
And that's basically, I think, the future of media also is people will find out what they believe about stuff basically by going, okay, I'm interested in this.
Okay, what's the info on that?
And as far as, you know, current events and stuff, you would just, uh, sign up for, uh, uh, an alert on a certain issue.
Uh, if a, if a bill goes through, then you're going to get the email and then you'll get to vote on it even.
Um, but basically when I look at the media and I listen to these people talk, you've got like Glenn Beck and Rush Limbaugh.

And that other guy who likes to scream and yell at people on Fox News, they, they just talk about like, um, they just like, like when they go up against each other, they just, they just go up against each other.
They're like, like labeling each other labels and, um, and saying vague stuff.
By the end, they're not, they're not, not really saying anything about an actual issue.

Um, again, I probably seem like a total idiot to some people watching this because I don't watch enough media and I should probably sit through a half hour long show before I upload a video talking about it.
Um, but I just had to say this.
Yeah.
It's just like, like when they do a discourse on president Obama, like I subscribed to white house before and I'll try to get in the habit of listening to what they say.
But instead of saying what the, what they said, they kind of say what the president said and what's going on.

And they kind of say like how it'll be perceived, um, like telling us how it'll be perceived and rather, rather than, and I know, I know, um, the reporters are supposed to be unbiased, but so like how, how is bias bias isn't going to be in the future of media?
Um, yeah, I think in the future media isn't going to be just, uh, you're going to have, you're going to be able to change.
You're going to choose what, what, what group you get your information from and whoever gives out information, they're going to be a lot more biased because they work for themselves.
They have their constituents and, um, and just whoever vibes with whoever will be with that, uh, that group.
And then the reporters out in the field won't be biased though.
But, uh, but anyways, in the future, everybody's going to be educated enough that there's not going to be so many disparate groups.
But, uh, it's really amazing to think about.
I'll probably do another video talking about it.
I hope this made sense.
Maybe I just rambled.
Anyways, that's what I think about the future of the media.
